items.title: detect_available_devices
items.text:
def detect_available_devices() -> list[str]

Detect available compute devices for transcription.

Detect available compute devices for transcription.

Returns devices based on available engines:
- cpu: Always available (WhisperX)
- cuda: Available if NVIDIA GPU + torch (WhisperX)
- mps: Available if Apple Silicon + mlx-whisper (MLX Whisper)

Result is cached.

Returns: list[str]

items.title: detect_diarization_device
items.text:
def detect_diarization_device() -> str

Detect the best device for pyannote diarization.

Detect the best device for pyannote diarization.

Pyannote supports cpu, cuda, and mps (Apple Silicon).
Returns the best available device.

Returns: str

items.title: detect_whisperx_device
items.text:
def detect_whisperx_device() -> str

Detect the best device for WhisperX transcription.

Detect the best device for WhisperX transcription.

WhisperX (ctranslate2) supports cpu and cuda only.
Uses CTranslate2's native CUDA detection (independent of PyTorch CUDA).
MPS is handled by engine auto-detect selecting mlx-whisper instead.

Returns: str

Returns:

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description device str Compute device (cpu, cuda)

Returns: str Optimal compute type: float16 for cuda, int8 for cpu.

Detect optimal GPU layer offload for llama.cpp.

Detect optimal GPU layer offload for llama.cpp.

    -1 (all layers on GPU) for Apple Silicon or CUDA systems,
    0 (CPU only) otherwise.

items.text: Source: docs/plans/2026-02-17-chunked-ai-context-settings.md

items.title: Task 1: Add max_context to Model Catalog + AI Settings Persistence
Allowed context sizes: powers-of-2 friendly stops from 2K to 128K
RAM estimates per context size (approximate, for UI display)
Based on Q4_K_M 8B model: ~5GB base + ~0.5GB per 8K context
Task 3: Context Window Truncation in API Routes (ask + chat/multi)
Task 4: Frontend — Context Window Slider in Settings
Task 5: Integration — Load Persisted AI Settings on Startup

items.title: Frequently Asked Questions
items.text:
What is Verbatim Studio and why would I use it?

Verbatim Studio is a privacy-first desktop application that lets you capture, transcribe, and search audio or video recordings without ever sending your data to the cloud. Think of it as your personal, offline “recording + AI-scribe” toolkit that runs entirely on your Mac, Windows, or Linux machine.

Typical use-cases - Students who want to record lectures and instantly search them. - Journalists who need accurate, time-stamped transcripts. - Teams that want meeting minutes but can’t upload sensitive audio to third-party services. - Anyone who hates subscription fees and wants to keep their data on their own disk.

Key promises - No Internet required after installation. - No telemetry or hidden uploads. - One-click export to Markdown, JSON, SRT, or plain text. - Tiny footprint: the whole app is < 200 MB and uses the CPU/GPU you already own.

???

How do I install Verbatim Studio?
  1. Download the latest release for your OS from GitHub Releases.
  2. Windows: Verbatim-Studio-Setup-x64.exe
  3. macOS: Verbatim-Studio.dmg

  4. Linux: Verbatim-Studio.AppImage (or .deb if you prefer)

  5. Run the installer.

  6. Windows & macOS: drag-and-drop or next-next-finish.

  7. Linux: 

    chmod +x Verbatim-Studio.AppImage
./Verbatim-Studio.AppImage

  8. First-launch setup wizard

  9. Choose language model size (Tiny = 30 MB, Base = 150 MB, Large = 1.4 GB).
  10. Pick a default folder for storing recordings (you can change this later).
  11. That’s it—no account, no e-mail, no license key.

Need the bleeding-edge version? Build from source:

git clone https://github.com/verbatim-studio/verbatim-studio.git
cd verbatim-studio
npm install
npm run dist

The resulting installer will be in dist/.

???

How do I make my first recording?
  1. Open Verbatim Studio and click the big red “Record” button (or press Ctrl+R).
  2. Choose microphone and quality (48 kHz is default; 16 kHz saves 50 % disk).
  3. Speak. The live waveform shows you audio is being captured.
  4. Press “Stop” (Esc). A new card appears in the library.

Transcribe automatically - Toggle “Auto-transcribe on stop” in Settings → Workflow. - Or right-click the card → “Transcribe”. - Progress bar appears; when finished, click the card to read or edit the transcript.

Keyboard shortcuts - Space = play/pause - Ctrl+S = save current edits - Ctrl+F = search inside transcript

???

Which languages and models are supported?

Out-of-the-box models (Whisper-compatible)

Model Disk RAM Languages Good for tiny 30 MB 400 MB 99 Quick drafts base 150 MB 600 MB 99 Everyday use large 1.4 GB 1.5 GB 99 Highest accuracy

You can add custom models: 1. Drop any Whisper .bin or .pt file into - Windows: %APPDATA%\Verbatim Studio\models - macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/Verbatim Studio/models - Linux: ~/.config/Verbatim Studio/models 2. Restart the app; the model appears in Settings → Transcription → Model dropdown.

Language auto-detection is enabled by default. To force one language: Settings → Transcription → Language → “es” (Spanish), “fr”, etc.

???

Where does Verbatim Studio store my files and how do I back them up?

Default storage folders - Windows: %USERPROFILE%\Documents\Verbatim Studio - macOS: ~/Documents/Verbatim Studio - Linux: ~/VerbatimStudio

Inside you will find 

recordings/          # raw WAV/MP4
transcripts/         # JSON + Markdown
db/                  # SQLite index (search cache)
thumbs/              # waveform thumbnails

Back-up strategies 1. Copy the entire folder to an external drive. 2. Use the built-in “Export Project” button (creates a single .vstudio zip). 3. Sync only the db and transcripts folders to cloud storage if you wish—audio files can be re-generated from transcripts if you still have the original media.

Portable mode Create a file named portable (no extension) next to the executable; all data will live in ./data relative to the binary—handy for USB sticks.

???

I’m getting a ‘StorageUnavailableError’—what should I do?

The app cannot write to its data directory. Quick checklist:

  1. Disk space
  2. At least 1 GB free is recommended.

  3. Check with df -h (Linux/macOS) or dir C:\ (Windows).

  4. Permissions

  5. Windows: right-click the app → “Run as administrator” once to create folders, then revert to normal user.

  6. macOS/Linux: 

    sudo chown -R $USER ~/Documents/Verbatim\ Studio

  7. Antivirus / Ransomware protection

  8. Add the data folder to the whitelist (Windows Defender, Malwarebytes, etc.).

  9. Portable mode on a read-only drive

  10. Move the AppImage/executable to a writable location or remove the portable flag.

Still stuck? Enable debug logging: Settings → Advanced → Log level → Debug, restart, and send the log file to support.

???

Can I edit the transcript after it’s generated?

Yes—double-click any word or paragraph. Changes are auto-saved every 5 seconds.

Time-code integrity - Edits never shift existing timestamps; only the text changes. - If you need to re-align, right-click → “Re-align timestamps” (re-runs forced-alignment on the audio snippet).

Find & replace - Ctrl+H opens a pane with regex support. - Replacement preview shows hits in yellow; press “Replace all” when happy.

Speaker labels - Type @John: at the start of a line; the app will remember “John” for the session. - Export formats (Markdown, SRT) respect these labels.

Spell-check - Enabled by default; add custom words via right-click → “Add to dictionary”.

???

How do I export or share my work?

Select one or more cards in the library, then:

Format Extension Includes Use-case Markdown .md Text + timestamps Blog post, Obsidian JSON .json Full word-level data Backup, programmatic use SRT .srt Subtitles Video editing Text .txt Plain text Quick read Project .vstudio Everything Archive, move to another PC

Bulk export - Hold Ctrl (or Cmd) to multi-select. - Settings → Export → “Merge into single file” creates one combined document.

Privacy tip - Exported files are plain text—encrypt before e-mailing. - The app never auto-uploads anything; you stay in control.

???

Is there a command-line interface or API?

Yes—Verbatim Studio ships with a headless binary vstudio-cli.

Basic usage 

# Transcribe a local file
vstudio-cli transcribe ./interview.wav --model base --language auto --out ./interview.md

# Record 30 seconds and transcribe
vstudio-cli record --duration 30 --format wav --transcribe

# Query the local database
vstudio-cli search "budget approval" --limit 10 --json

Built-in HTTP API (off by default) Enable in Settings → Developer → “Start local API server” (port 9780). Then: 

curl -X POST http://localhost:9780/api/v1/transcribe \
  -F "audio=@meeting.mp3" \
  -F "model=base"
Returns JSON with segments and confidence scores. Perfect for integrating with OBS, Automator, or Alfred workflows.

???

How can I contribute or report bugs?

We love community help!

  1. Bug reports

  2. Open an issue on GitHub and attach the debug log (Settings → Advanced → “Export log”).

  3. Translations

  4. Edit locales/*.json and submit a pull request.

  5. Code

  6. Fork, create a feature branch, run npm run test, then PR.
  7. Python backend tests: pytest tests/.

  8. TypeScript frontend tests: npm run test:ui.

  9. Documentation

  10. The docs folder is MkDocs. Preview locally: 

    pip install -r docs/requirements.txt
mkdocs serve

  11. Donations

  12. Sponsor

items.title: Verbatim‑Studio – End‑User Feature Guide
items.text: Welcome to Verbatim‑Studio, the all‑in‑one audio‑transcription and collaboration platform. This guide explains the most useful features you’ll encounter while using the app, from the simple UI widgets to the handy API calls that power the backend. All explanations are written in plain language, with examples that show how to get the most out of each feature.

items.title: Table of Contents
items.text:
1. Getting Started
  2. Core API Features
  3. Directory Size
  4. File Size
  5. Transcript Text
  6. User‑Facing Components
  7. App & DataSyncProvider
  8. Onboarding Tour & Tour Toast
  9. Update Prompt
  10. OAuth Connect Button
  11. Download & Task Indicators
  12. Audio Level Meter
  13. Plugin Slot
  14. Putting It All Together
  15. FAQs

items.title: 1. Getting Started
items.text:
1. Launch Verbatim‑Studio – Open the app from your desktop or browser.
  2. Create an Account – Sign up with your email or connect via a single‑click OAuth button (Google, Microsoft, etc.).
  3. Take Your First Recording – Click the big “Record” button, speak, and let the app transcribe in real time.
  4. Explore the Dashboard – View your projects, transcripts, and file sizes.

items.title: 2. Core API Features
items.text: The API powers the heavy lifting behind the scenes. While you don’t need to touch the code, it’s useful to know what each endpoint does, especially if you want to integrate Verbatim‑Studio with other tools.

items.title: Directory Size
items.text:
Endpoint Purpose Example GET /_dir_size Returns the total size (bytes) and number of files in a directory. curl https://api.verbatim-studio.com/_dir_size?path=/recordings → {"size": 12345678, "count": 42}

When to use it - Quickly check how much storage your project is using. - Automate cleanup scripts that delete old recordings.

items.title: File Size
items.text:
Endpoint Purpose Example GET /_file_size Returns the size (bytes) of a single file. curl https://api.verbatim-studio.com/_file_size?file=meeting.mp3 → {"size": 9876543}

When to use it - Verify that a newly uploaded file was transferred correctly. - Display file sizes in the UI without re‑loading the whole directory.

items.title: Transcript Text
items.text:
Endpoint Purpose Example GET /_transcript_text Retrieves the full transcript text from all segments of a recording. curl https://api.verbatim-studio.com/_transcript_text?file=meeting.mp3 → {"text":"Hello, this is the meeting transcript..."}

When to use it - Copy the entire transcript into a document or email. - Feed the text into a search engine or analytics tool.

items.title: 3. User‑Facing Components
items.text: These are the building blocks you interact with directly in the UI. Each component has a clear purpose and can be used in everyday workflows.

items.title: App & DataSyncProvider
items.text:
• App – The main container that holds everything you see on screen.
  • DataSyncProvider – Keeps your files, transcripts, and settings in sync across devices.

Practical tip When you open a project on a new device, the DataSyncProvider automatically pulls the latest recordings and transcripts so you never lose progress.

items.title: Onboarding Tour & Tour Toast
items.text:
• OnboardingTour – A step‑by‑step guide that walks you through the first time you use the app.
  • TourToast – Small pop‑up messages that appear during the tour to give quick hints.

When to use The first time you log in, the OnboardingTour will highlight the “Record” button, the “Library” tab, and the “Export” option. TourToast messages appear next to each highlighted area.

items.title: Update Prompt
items.text: When a new version of Verbatim‑Studio is available, the Update Prompt appears in the top‑right corner. - Click Update Now to download the latest features. - Click Later to postpone the update.

items.title: OAuth Connect Button
items.text:
A single button that lets you connect to external services (Google Drive, Dropbox, Microsoft OneDrive, etc.).

Example 1. Click Connect to Google Drive. 2. Grant permission in the popup. 3. Your recordings will automatically sync to the chosen folder.

items.title: Download & Task Indicators
items.text:
• DownloadIndicator – Shows a progress bar when you’re downloading a file or a large transcript.
  • TaskIndicator – Displays the status of background jobs (e.g., “Transcribing…”, “Uploading…”).

Practical use When you click “Export Transcript”, the DownloadIndicator shows a spinning wheel until the file is ready. The TaskIndicator will say “Exporting” while the process runs.

items.title: Audio Level Meter
items.text:
A real‑time visual display of the audio level while you’re recording.

Why it matters - Ensures your voice is loud enough and not too quiet. - Helps avoid clipping (when the audio is too loud).

items.title: Plugin Slot
items.text:
A placeholder in the UI where third‑party plugins can inject their own controls or views.

Example If you install the “Highlight Words” plugin, a new button will appear in the Plugin Slot that lets you tag important phrases.

items.title: Scenario 1 – Recording a Meeting
items.text:
1. Start Recording – Click the big red button.
  2. Watch the Audio Level Meter – Make sure the bars stay in the green zone.
  3. Stop & Transcribe – The app automatically starts transcribing.
  4. Check Progress – The TaskIndicator will show “Transcribing…”.
  5. Export – Click Export → a DownloadIndicator pops up while the PDF is generated.
  6. Share – Use the OAuth Connect Button to send the PDF to Google Drive.

items.title: Scenario 2 – Managing Storage
items.text:
1. Open the Library – View all recordings.
  2. Check Directory Size – Use the API (or the built‑in “Storage” tab) to see that your folder is 1.2 GB.
  3. Delete Old Files – Remove files that are no longer needed.
  4. Sync – DataSyncProvider updates all devices instantly.

items.title: 5. FAQs
items.text:
Question Answer Do I need to know the API to use Verbatim‑Studio? No. The API is for advanced integrations. All core features are available through the UI. Can I connect to multiple cloud services? Yes. Use the OAuth Connect Button for each service you want to sync with. What happens if my recording is too large? The app will show a warning and offer to compress or split the file. How do I install plugins? Go to **Settings

items.title: Getting Started
items.text:
Privacy-first transcription for professionals

This guide covers the prerequisites, installation, and project structure for verbatim-studio.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• TypeScript development environment
  • Python development environment
  • Markdown development environment
  • Shell development environment
  • JSON development environment
  • YAML development environment
  • JavaScript development environment
  • TOML development environment

items.title: Installation
items.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio
cd verbatim-studio

# Install dependencies
poetry install

items.title: Code Insights
items.text: Automated analysis findings and improvement suggestions.

items.title: Summary
items.text: Severity Count  Critical 0  Warning 5  Info 1 Total 6

items.title: Documentation
items.text:
2138 undocumented exported symbols

Found 2138 exported symbols without JSDoc/docstring documentation across 193 files. Examples: RecordingTemplateInfo, Recording, Tag, TagListResponse, UniqueSpeaker....

Affected files: packages/frontend/src/lib/api.ts, packages/backend/api/routes/system.py, packages/backend/api/routes/storage_locations.py, packages/backend/api/routes/ai.py, packages/backend/api/routes/config.py, packages/backend/api/routes/recordings.py, packages/backend/api/routes/speakers.py, packages/backend/api/routes/search.py, packages/backend/api/routes/transcripts.py, packages/backend/api/routes/projects.py

Suggestion: Add JSDoc comments or docstrings to all exported symbols to improve API documentation quality.

AI Analysis

To address the issue of 2138 undocumented exported symbols, I recommend adding JSDoc comments to all exported functions, classes, and variables. For example, in packages/frontend/src/lib/api.ts, add a JSDoc comment to the getAuthToken function: 

/**
 * Retrieves the stored auth token.
 * @returns The auth token or null if not stored.
 */
export function getAuthToken(): string | null {
  return localStorage.getItem(AUTH_TOKEN_KEY);
}
This will improve API documentation quality and help other developers understand the purpose and usage of the exported symbols.

items.title: Code Quality
items.text:
62 oversized modules

Found 62 modules exceeding 500 lines or 30 symbols. Largest: packages/frontend/src/lib/api.ts (2802 lines, 167 symbols).

Affected files: packages/frontend/src/lib/api.ts, packages/backend/api/routes/system.py, packages/backend/api/routes/storage_locations.py, packages/backend/api/routes/ai.py, packages/backend/api/routes/recordings.py, packages/backend/api/routes/search.py, packages/backend/api/routes/transcripts.py, packages/backend/api/routes/projects.py, packages/backend/api/routes/browse.py, packages/backend/api/routes/documents.py

Suggestion: Consider splitting large modules into smaller, focused files with single responsibility.

AI Analysis

Fix Recommendation:

To address the oversized module issue, we'll break down the large api.ts file into smaller, focused files with single responsibility. We'll create separate files for each functionality:

  1. api-config.ts: Handle API URL configuration, caching, and injection.
  2. auth.ts: Manage auth tokens, including storage, retrieval, and clearing.
  3. api-url.ts: Handle API URL initialization, including Electron and fallback logic.
  4. api-utils.ts: Move utility functions, such as logging and exponential backoff.

Updated File Structure:

packages/frontend/src/lib/
api-config.ts
auth.ts
api-url.ts
api-utils.ts

Example Code:

api-config.ts: 

// Extend window type for injected API URL
declare global {
  interface Window {
    __VERBATIM_API_URL__?: string;
  }
}

// Cache the API URL once resolved
let cachedApiBaseUrl: string | null = null;
let apiUrlPromise: Promise<string> | null = null;

auth.ts: ```typescript const AUTH_TOKEN_KEY = 'verbatim_token'; const AUTH_REFRESH_KEY = 'verbatim_refresh_token';

let _onAuthRequired: (() => void) | null = null;

/* Register a callback invoked when the server returns 401. / export function onAuthRequired(cb: () => void) { _onAuthRequired = cb; }

/** Store auth

253 orphan modules (potential dead code)

Found 253 modules with no imports or exports in the dependency graph. These may be unused dead code.

Affected files: CLAUDE_CONTEXT.md, README.md, docs/DEPENDENCIES.md, docs/ROADMAP.md, docs/VERBATIM_FEATURE_MATRIX.md, docs/VERBATIM_STUDIO_SPEC_v2.md, docs/architecture/enterprise-strategy.md, docs/architecture/plugin-system-design.md, docs/cloud-storage-setup.md, docs/electron-code-signing.md

Suggestion: Review orphan modules — remove if unused, or add explicit imports/exports if they are needed.

48 symbols with missing or any types

Found 48 exported functions with any types or missing return types. Examples: clearAuthTokens, useUploadDocument, useUpdateDocument.

Affected files: packages/frontend/src/lib/api.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useDocuments.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useRecordings.ts, packages/frontend/src/stores/taskStore.ts, packages/frontend/src/stores/downloadStore.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useProjects.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useConversations.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useDashboard.ts, packages/frontend/src/hooks/useSearchHistory.ts, packages/frontend/src/components/updates/UpdatePrompt.tsx

Suggestion: Add explicit type annotations to improve type safety and documentation quality.

Mixed naming conventions across exports

Exports use multiple naming conventions: PascalCase (676), camelCase (205), snake_case (136). The dominant convention is PascalCase.

Suggestion: Consider standardizing on PascalCase for consistency across the codebase.

AI Analysis

Pick one convention (PascalCase is already the majority) and enforce it at the lint level.

  1. Add an ESLint rule: 
    "unicorn/filename-case": ["error", { "case": "pascalCase" }]
  2. Rename the 341 non-conforming files in one scripted pass: 
    find src -name '*.js' | xargs -I {} bash -c 'mv "$1" "$(echo "$1" | sed -E "s/\/([a-z]+(_|-)[a-z]+)/\/\U\1/g")"' _ {}
  3. Commit the renames and the rule together; any future PR that breaks the convention will fail CI.

items.title: Architecture
items.text:
103 deeply nested files

Found 103 files nested more than 5 directories deep. Deepest: packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/repositories.py (6 levels).

Affected files: packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/repositories.py, packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/mappers.py, packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/adapter.py, packages/frontend/src/components/recordings/RecordingSetupPanel.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/updates/WhatsNewDialog.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/updates/UpdatePrompt.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/ai/AttachmentPicker.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/documents/DocumentFilters.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/recordings/RecordingFilters.tsx, packages/frontend/src/components/live/MetadataPanel.tsx

Suggestion: Consider flattening the directory structure to reduce import path complexity.

AI Analysis

Fix Move the three SQLite‑specific files (repositories.py, mappers.py, adapter.py) into a single sqlite package and expose them via __init__.py. Then import them from the parent package (packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite) instead of a 6‑level path. This reduces depth and keeps the import graph flat.

# packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/__init__.py

items.title: Verbatim‑Studio Troubleshooting Guide
items.text: For end‑users – quick, friendly help when things go sideways.

items.title: 1. Quick‑Start
items.text:
Step What to do Why it matters 1️⃣ Install pip install verbatim-studio (Python) or npm i verbatim-studio (Node) Gets the latest stable binaries. 2️⃣ Verify verbatim-studio --version Confirms the tool is reachable. 3️⃣ Configure Edit ~/.verbatim-studio/config.yaml (or config.json) Sets storage paths, auth tokens, etc. 4️⃣ Run verbatim-studio process myfile.txt Starts a document‑processing job.

Tip – If you’re new, run verbatim-studio help to see all commands and options.

items.title: 2. Common Issues & Fixes
items.text:
Below are the most frequently reported errors. For each, we explain what triggers it and give a step‑by‑step fix.

⚠️ StorageError – Base storage error. Cause – A generic failure when accessing the storage backend. Fix – Check the detailed sub‑error (see below) or try a fresh connection.

items.title: 2.1 StorageUnavailableError
items.text:
What it means The storage location (e.g., a cloud bucket or a local folder) cannot be reached. Typical symptoms “Unable to connect to storage” Timeout or DNS resolution failures

Steps to fix

  1. Ping the storage host 
    ping <storage-host>
  2. Check network connectivity 
    curl -I https://<storage-host>
  3. Verify that the storage service is running (e.g., S3, GCS, local NFS).
  4. If using a VPN or proxy, ensure it’s active and that the required ports are open.

✅ If the host is reachable, try restarting Verbatim‑Studio or re‑authenticating.

items.title: 2.2 StorageConnectionError
items.text:
What it means The client could not establish a connection to the storage backend. Typical symptoms “Connection refused” “Failed to open socket”

Steps to fix

  1. Confirm the endpoint URL in config.yaml.
  2. Test the connection manually 
    curl -v https://<storage-endpoint>
  3. Check firewall rules – ensure outbound traffic to the endpoint is allowed.
  4. If using TLS, verify certificates (openssl s_client -connect <host>:443).

⚠️ If you’re behind a corporate proxy, set HTTPS_PROXY and HTTP_PROXY environment variables.

items.title: 2.3 StorageAuthError
items.text:
What it means Authentication to the storage service failed or the token has expired. Typical symptoms “401 Unauthorized” “Invalid credentials”

Steps to fix

  1. Refresh your credentials
  2. For AWS: aws configure
  3. For GCP: gcloud auth login
  4. For local services: re‑enter username/password in config.yaml
  5. Verify the token’s expiry (date -d @<epoch>).
  6. Check the scope/permissions – the account must have read/write rights to the bucket/folder.

✅ After updating credentials, restart the process.

items.title: 2.4 StorageNotFoundError
items.text:
What it means The requested file or directory does not exist in the storage location. Typical symptoms “File not found” “404 Not Found”

Steps to fix

  1. Double‑check the path in your command or config.
  2. List the contents of the target folder to confirm the file’s presence. 
    verbatim-studio ls <bucket-or-folder>
  3. If the file is missing, upload it or correct the path.

⚠️ Remember that paths are case‑sensitive on most storage backends.

items.title: 2.5 StoragePermissionError
items.text:
What it means The user/account lacks the necessary permissions to read or write. Typical symptoms “403 Forbidden” “Permission denied”

Steps to fix

  1. Check IAM policies (AWS IAM, GCP IAM, etc.) for the bucket/folder.
  2. Grant the required role (e.g., storage.objectAdmin).
  3. If using a local filesystem, ensure the file permissions allow the user running Verbatim‑Studio. 
    ls -l /path/to/file
chmod 664 /path/to/file

✅ After updating permissions, retry the operation.

items.title: 2.6 ProcessingCancelledError
items.text:
What it means The document processing job was cancelled by the user or by an external trigger. Typical symptoms “Processing cancelled” Job stops mid‑way

Steps to fix

  1. Check if you pressed Ctrl+C or sent a SIGTERM – if so, that’s expected.
  2. If cancellation is unexpected, look for a cancel flag in your config or a background job manager that might be killing the process.
  3. Restart the job with verbatim-studio process myfile.txt.

⚠️ If you’re running in a CI pipeline, ensure the job timeout is long enough.

items.title: 3. General Troubleshooting
items.text: Area What to check How to check Installation Correct package version verbatim-studio --version Dependencies All required libraries are installed pip list | grep verbatim-studio Configuration config.yaml syntax is valid yamllint config.yaml Environment Variables like VERBATIM_TOKEN are set env | grep VERBATIM Logs Look for detailed error messages verbatim-studio --log-level debug

items.title: 3.1 Common “I can’t get it to start” problems
items.text:
• Missing dependencies – run pip install -r requirements.txt or npm install.
  • Permission denied on config file – chmod 600 ~/.verbatim-studio/config.yaml.
  • Port conflicts – if the tool uses a local server, ensure the port isn’t taken (lsof -i :<port>).

items.title: 4. Getting Help
items.text:
Resource What it offers How to access Official Documentation Full API reference, advanced config https://verbatim-studio.docs/ Community Forum Q&A, user tips https://forum.verbatim-studio.io GitHub Issues Report bugs, request features https://github.com/verbatim-studio/verbatim-studio/issues Support Ticket Direct help from the dev team verbatim-studio support or email support@verbatim-studio.io

Pro tip – When filing an issue, include the exact error message, your OS, and the output of verbatim-studio --debug.

items.title: 5. Final Words
items.text:
We know tech hiccups can be frustrating, but most of the time the solution is a quick check of your config or a simple credential refresh. If you’re still stuck after following this guide, don’t hesitate to reach out through one of the channels above. Happy processing! 🚀

items.title: Getting Started with Verbatim Studio
items.text: Welcome to Verbatim Studio! This guide will help you install the software and try it out for the first time. Let's get started!

items.title: Step 1: Install Verbatim Studio
items.text:
1. Download the software Visit the official Verbatim Studio website and download the installer for your operating system (Windows, macOS, or Linux).

  2. Run the installer

  3. Windows: Double-click the .exe file and follow the on-screen instructions.
  4. macOS: Open the .dmg file and drag the app to your Applications folder.

  5. Linux: Extract the downloaded archive and run the installation script (look for a .sh file).

  6. Launch Verbatim Studio Once installed, open the application from your desktop or applications menu.

items.title: Step 2: First Use Walkthrough
items.text: Let’s try a simple example to see how Verbatim Studio works. We’ll use its built-in tools to check file sizes and view transcripts.

items.title: Example: Check File Size
items.text:
1. Prepare a file Place a file you want to analyze (e.g., example.txt) in the uploads folder inside Verbatim Studio’s directory.

  2. Open your browser In your web browser, visit this URL: 

    http://localhost:8080/_file_size?file=example.txt
    *(Replace example.txt

items.title: Verbatim‑Studio – Your Voice, Your Workflow
items.text: Verbatim‑Studio is a simple, all‑in‑one workspace that lets you record, edit, and organize spoken content right from your computer or mobile device. Think of it as a digital notebook for anything you say—interviews, podcasts, meeting notes, language practice, or personal journals. Everything you capture stays on your device, and you decide how it’s used, shared, or stored.

items.title: Who will love Verbatim‑Studio?
items.text:
• Podcasters & interviewers who need a reliable way to capture high‑quality audio and add quick notes while recording.
  • Students & researchers who want to turn spoken ideas into searchable text without juggling multiple apps.
  • Teams and remote workers looking for an easy way to record meeting highlights and turn them into action items.
  • Anyone who prefers speaking over typing and wants a tidy place to keep those recordings organized and safe.

In short, if you ever wish you could “talk” to your notes and have them neatly filed away, Verbatim‑Studio solves that problem—no extra hardware, no cloud‑only lock‑in, and no confusing tech steps.

items.title: What can you do with it?
items.text:
• Record & playback – Start a session with a single click, watch a visual audio level meter, and listen back instantly.
  • Live transcription & prompts – As you speak, the app can show a real‑time transcript and suggest prompts to keep the conversation flowing.
  • Organize with tags & folders – Drag recordings into custom collections, add simple labels, and find them later with a quick search.
  • Sync across devices – A built‑in sync helper keeps your library up‑to‑date whether you’re on a laptop, tablet, or phone.
  • Export & share – Download recordings as audio files, copy the transcript, or send a shareable link—all without leaving the app.
  • Guided onboarding – A friendly tour walks you through the main tools the first time you open the program, so you’re productive right away.

All of these features are presented with clean, intuitive buttons and clear visual cues, so you can focus on what you’re saying rather than how the software works. Give your voice the home it deserves—try Verbatim‑Studio today!

items.text:
Bootstrap module for copying bundled models to HuggingFace cache on first launch.

items.title: resource-migration.ts
items.text:
Migrates heavy resources (Python env, FFmpeg) from the app bundle to the

Migrates heavy resources (Python env, FFmpeg) from the app bundle to the user data directory on first launch. This allows stripped "update" releases to work without re-bundling the Python environment every time. Flow: Full install → Python in bundle → copied to user data on first launch Update install → No Python in bundle → user data copy already exists

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`apps/electron/src/main/resource-migration.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/apps/electron/src/main/resource-migration.ts) — this module (257 lines) Source apps/electron/src/main/resource-migration.ts Language TypeScript Lines 257 Exports 2

Generates a shell script for seamless macOS app replacement.

Generates a shell script for seamless macOS app replacement. The script: 1. Waits for the app to quit (with timeout) 2. Removes old app from /Applications 3. Copies new app from mounted DMG volume 4. Relaunches the app 5. Cleans up (unmounts DMG, removes temp files)

Name Type Default Description volumePath string appName string

items.title: updater.ts
items.text:
Orchestrates GitHub-based auto-updates for the Electron app by polling releases, downloading platform-specific assets, and executing in-place upgrades while preserving the Python runtime. Exports initUpdater(), checkForUpdates(), and installUpdate() to manage the full update lifecycle, storing timestamps and version metadata via update-store. Consumed by the main process to schedule daily checks and prompt users when a newer release is available, ensuring Verbatim Studio stays current without external package managers.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`apps/electron/src/main/updater.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/apps/electron/src/main/updater.ts) — this module (611 lines) - [`apps/electron/src/main/update-store.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/apps/electron/src/main/update-store.ts) - [`apps/electron/src/main/update-script.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/apps/electron/src/main/update-script.ts) Source apps/electron/src/main/updater.ts Language TypeScript Lines 611 Exports 6

items.title: llama_cpp.py
items.text:
Llama.cpp AI service adapter.

Llama.cpp AI service adapter.

Implements IAIService for local LLM inference using llama-cpp-python. Uses create_chat_completion() for correct chat template handling.

Includes singleton caching with automatic invalidation when model path changes.

items.title: get_llama_service
items.text:
def get_llama_service(

View source

Get a cached LlamaCppAIService, creating or replacing as needed.

Get a cached LlamaCppAIService, creating or replacing as needed.

If the model_path differs from the cached service's path, the cache
is invalidated and a new service is created. This allows model switching
without restarting the server.

Args:
    model_path: Path to the GGUF model file
    n_ctx: Context window size
    n_gpu_layers: Number of layers to offload to GPU

Returns:
    Cached or newly created LlamaCppAIService instance

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description model_path? str | None None Path to the GGUF model file n_ctx? int 4096 Context window size n_gpu_layers? int 0 Number of layers to offload to GPU

Returns: "LlamaCppAIService" Cached or newly created LlamaCppAIService instance

items.title: cleanup_llama_service
items.text:
def cleanup_llama_service() -> None

View source

Unload the cached LLM service to free memory.

Unload the cached LLM service to free memory.

Deletes the llama.cpp model from memory, clears the singleton cache,
and runs garbage collection. Frees ~4-5 GB for an 8B Q4 model.

Returns: None

items.title: SQLiteDatabaseAdapter
items.text:
View source

SQLite implementation of the database adapter.

SQLite implementation of the database adapter.

Manages database connection, session lifecycle, and provides
access to all repositories.

Usage:
    adapter = SQLiteDatabaseAdapter("sqlite+aiosqlite:///./app.db")
    await adapter.initialize()

    async with adapter.session() as session:
        project = await adapter.projects.get("123")

    await adapter.close()

items.title: mappers.py
items.text:
Entity mappers between SQLAlchemy models and domain entities.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/mappers.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/mappers.py) — this module (204 lines) Source packages/backend/adapters/database/sqlite/mappers.py Language Python Lines 204 Exports 14

AI Summary

{"module":"Bridges the impedance mismatch between SQLAlchemy persistence models and domain entities by exposing bidirectional converters for every aggregate root. Exposes paired to_entity/from_entity functions for Project, Recording, Transcript, Segment, Speaker, Job and Setting that the SQLite adapter calls before handing control to the repository layer. Keeps the core layer free of storage details while allowing the adapter to honor database-specific column names like metadata_.","project_to_entity":"Hydrates a ProjectEntity from a SQLAlchemy Project row when the repository reads data.","entity_to_project":"Persists or updates a Project row from a ProjectEntity, optionally reusing an existing model instance.","recording_to_entity":"Maps a Recording row to a RecordingEntity so the domain can work with file metadata and status.","entity_to_recording":"Writes a RecordingEntity back to the database, creating or updating the Recording row.","transcript_to_entity":"Converts a Transcript row into a TranscriptEntity carrying language, model and confidence metrics.","entity_to_transcript":"Serializes a TranscriptEntity to the Transcript table, preserving computed statistics.","segment_to_entity":"Transforms a Segment row into a SegmentEntity with timing, text and speaker information.","entity_to_segment":"Stores a SegmentEntity

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/api/routes/ai.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/api/routes/ai.py) — this module (1167 lines) Source packages/backend/api/routes/ai.py Language Python Lines 1167 Exports 17

AI Summary

{"module":"FastAPI router that exposes REST endpoints for LLM chat, summarization, and document analysis while managing model lifecycle through on-demand dependency installation and active model tracking. Exposes endpoints like /chat, /summarize, /analyze, /models, and /install that integrate with core.interfaces.ChatMessage, core.factory, and persistence.models to provide streaming and batch AI operations. Consumed by the frontend React app to deliver AI-powered features and by the CLI for model management operations.","ChatRequest":"Request body for single-turn chat containing the user message, optional system prompt, and chat options like temperature and max tokens.","ChatResponse":"Response wrapper for chat containing the assistant's reply message and usage statistics.","HistoryMessage":"Represents a single message in chat history with role and content fields for maintaining conversation context.","MultiChatRequest":"Request body for multi-turn chat containing an array of history messages plus the next user message.","StreamToken":"SSE payload for streaming chat responses containing either a token chunk or final usage metadata.","SummarizationResponse":"Response wrapper for text summarization containing the generated summary and original text length.","AnalysisResponse":"Response wrapper for document analysis containing extracted insights, key topics, and action items.","AIStatusResponse":"Status

items.title: diarization.py
items.text:
Diarization model management API routes.

Diarization model management API routes.

Provides endpoints for listing and downloading pyannote models required for speaker diarization. These models are gated and require HuggingFace authentication.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/api/routes/diarization.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/api/routes/diarization.py) — this module (237 lines) Source packages/backend/api/routes/diarization.py Language Python Lines 237 Exports 4

AI Summary

Exposes REST endpoints for managing pyannote speaker-diarization models by listing gated HuggingFace repos, reporting download status, and streaming multi-repo downloads into the torch/pyannote cache so the transcription pipeline can run offline. Key exports are the Pydantic response models DiarizationModelResponse and DiarizationModelsResponse plus the FastAPI router mounted at /diarization, all consumed by the frontend settings UI to drive the model-download workflow.

Defines REST endpoints for managing transcript segment highlights via two FastAPI routers: segment_highlights_router handles individual highlight operations at /segments/{id}/highlight while transcript_highlights_router supports bulk operations at /transcripts/{id}/bulk-highlight. Exposes Pydantic models HighlightResponse, HighlightSetRequest, BulkHighlightRequest, and MessageResponse to validate requests and serialize responses. Integrates with the persistence layer through SQLAlchemy models SegmentHighlight and Segment, enabling the frontend to color-code transcript segments for review workflows.

items.text:
Live transcription WebSocket endpoint.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/api/routes/live.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/api/routes/live.py) — this module (637 lines) Source packages/backend/api/routes/live.py Language Python Lines 637 Exports 10

AI Summary

Manages real-time WebSocket transcription sessions by maintaining in-memory LiveSession objects that buffer audio chunks and segments, exposes endpoints for saving completed sessions to the database as Recording/Transcript records, and broadcasts progress updates via the sync layer to connected clients.

items.title: notes.py
items.text:
Notes API endpoints for document and recording annotations.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/api/routes/notes.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/api/routes/notes.py) — this module (216 lines) Source packages/backend/api/routes/notes.py Language Python Lines 216 Exports 6

AI Summary

Defines the REST interface for annotation operations, exposing CRUD endpoints under /notes that accept NoteCreate/NoteUpdate payloads and return NoteResponse envelopes. Orchestrates annotation persistence by joining Note records with Document or Recording entities via SQLAlchemy async queries, enforcing that each note anchors to exactly one parent resource. Consumed by the frontend annotation panel to create timestamped or page-linked notes, and by the search indexer to surface anchored insights within media transcripts.

AI Summary

Okay, let's tackle this. The user wants a JSON summary of the stats.py module. First, I need to understand what this file does. The file is part of the backend API, specifically handling statistics endpoints for a dashboard. The imports include FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, and other modules related to the database and services.

Looking at the code, there's an APIRouter set up with a prefix /stats. The key exports are several Pydantic models like RecordingStats, TranscriptionStats, etc., and a DashboardStats class that combines them. The get_stats function is an endpoint that aggregates data from the database using SQLAlchemy queries, applying filters based on storage location. It calculates various stats like counts, sums, and averages for recordings, transcriptions, projects, processing jobs, and documents.

The module's architectural role is to provide a centralized endpoint for dashboard statistics by aggregating data from different models. The key exports are the Pydantic models that structure the statistical data. Each model represents a specific category of stats. The DashboardStats combines all these into a single response. The get_stats function uses the database session and interacts with the storage service to apply location-based filters, connecting with other parts of the system like the persistence layer and services.

Now, for

AI Summary

{"module":"Orchestrates all transcript-related HTTP operations by exposing REST endpoints that fetch, update, and export transcript data with segments, speaker stats, and AI summaries. Exposes SegmentResponse, TranscriptResponse, TranscriptWithSegmentsResponse, and ExportFormat models plus router handlers like get_transcript_with_segments(), update_segment(), and export_transcript() that join persistence.models with services.export. Consumed by the FastAPI app layer to serve the web dashboard and downstream clients needing structured transcript output.","SegmentResponse":"Shapes the JSON returned for a single segment, including computed fields like highlight_color and comment_count that are hydrated by _load_segment_annotations().","AISummary":"Carries the AI-generated summary block returned when a transcript includes key_points, action_items, topics, and named_entities.","TranscriptResponse":"Defines the core transcript metadata returned by list and detail endpoints, excluding the heavy segments collection.","TranscriptWithSegmentsResponse":"Extends TranscriptResponse to embed the full segments list for endpoints that need to return a complete transcript graph in one request.","SegmentUpdateRequest":"Validates the optional text and speaker fields that callers may patch when correcting a segment.","BulkDeleteRequest":"Validates the array of segment IDs sent to the bulk-delete endpoint

items.text:
Whisper model management API routes.

Whisper model management API routes.

Provides endpoints for listing, downloading, activating, and deleting Whisper models for transcription (MLX on macOS, CTranslate2 on Windows).

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/api/routes/whisper.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/api/routes/whisper.py) — this module (276 lines) Source packages/backend/api/routes/whisper.py Language Python Lines 276 Exports 4

AI Summary

Orchestrates Whisper model lifecycle by exposing REST endpoints that list, download, activate, and delete MLX/CTranslate2 models. Exposes list_whisper_models() and download_whisper_model() to the frontend, coordinating with core.whisper_catalog for disk state and core.transcription_settings to persist the active model choice. Consumed by the React settings UI to let users switch transcription engines without restarting the backend.

items.title: events.py
items.text:
Lightweight async event bus for plugin integration.

Lightweight async event bus for plugin integration.

Core code emits events at key moments (transcription complete, document uploaded, etc.). Plugins subscribe to events in their register() method.

Usage: from core.events import on, emit, clear

async def my_handler(**kwargs):
    print(kwargs)

on("transcription.complete", my_handler)
await emit("transcription.complete", transcript_id="abc", recording_id="xyz")
Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/core/events.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/core/events.py) — this module (43 lines) Source packages/backend/core/events.py Language Python Lines 43 Exports 5

items.title: factory.py
items.text:
Adapter factory for dependency injection.

Adapter factory for dependency injection.

Provides the correct adapter implementations based on the configured tier. Basic tier uses embedded services (SQLite, WhisperX local, llama.cpp). Enterprise tier uses external services (PostgreSQL, Ollama, Redis queue).

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/core/factory.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/core/factory.py) — this module (394 lines) - [`packages/frontend/tailwind.config.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/frontend/tailwind.config.ts) Source packages/backend/core/factory.py Language Python Lines 394 Exports 5

AI Summary

Central dependency injection hub that selects SQLite/WhisperX local/llama.cpp for basic tier or PostgreSQL/Ollama/Redis for enterprise tier. Exports AdapterFactory, create_factory_from_settings(), get_factory(), and create_transcription_engine_from_settings() to instantiate the correct adapters based on runtime configuration. Consumed by the CLI layer and FastAPI app to wire up the appropriate infrastructure stack.

items.title: AdapterFactory
items.text:
View source

Factory for creating adapter instances based on tier configuration.

Factory for creating adapter instances based on tier configuration.

Usage:
    from core.config import settings
    from core.factory import AdapterFactory, AdapterConfig

    config = AdapterConfig(database_url=settings.DATABASE_URL)
    factory = AdapterFactory(settings.MODE, config)

    db = await factory.create_database_adapter()
    transcription = factory.create_transcription_engine()
    auth = factory.create_auth_provider()

items.title: pyannote_catalog.py
items.text:
Pyannote model catalog for speaker diarization.

Pyannote model catalog for speaker diarization.

Defines the pyannote models required for speaker diarization. The diarization pipeline (speaker-diarization-3.1) is a config-only repo that orchestrates two neural network sub-models: - pyannote/segmentation-3.0 (5.9 MB, gated) - pyannote/wespeaker-voxceleb-resnet34-LM (26.6 MB, public)

All gated models require a HuggingFace token with accepted license agreements. Downloads are managed explicitly — no auto-downloads at runtime.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/core/pyannote_catalog.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/core/pyannote_catalog.py) — this module (164 lines) Source packages/backend/core/pyannote_catalog.py Language Python Lines 164 Exports 12

Detect available compute devices for transcription.

items.title: whisper_catalog.py
items.text:
Whisper model catalog for transcription.

Whisper model catalog for transcription.

Defines available whisper models that can be downloaded and used for transcription. Supports MLX-format models (macOS) and CTranslate2-format models (Windows/Linux).

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/core/whisper_catalog.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/core/whisper_catalog.py) — this module (224 lines) Source packages/backend/core/whisper_catalog.py Language Python Lines 224 Exports 12

AI Summary

Okay, let's start by understanding the file's content. The module is called whisper_catalog.py, and it's part of the backend core. The main purpose seems to be managing available Whisper models for transcription. It defines models in two formats: MLX for macOS and CTranslate2 for other platforms.

First, the module imports necessary libraries like sys, pathlib, and typing. The key exports include the WhisperModel class, which is a TypedDict with specific fields. Then there are two lists of models: WHISPER_MODELS and WHISPER_MODELS_CT2, each containing different model configurations for different platforms.

Looking at the exported functions, there's get_platform_models which returns the appropriate model list based on the platform. Functions like get_whisper_model and get_default_whisper_model help retrieve specific models or the default one. Other functions deal with cache directories, checking if a model is downloaded, getting model sizes, and converting model IDs to repositories.

The module's architectural role is to catalog available Whisper models, providing access to their metadata and paths. It connects to the broader system by supplying model info to the download and transcription modules. The key exports are the model definitions and functions that manage model selection and caching.

Now, for each symbol, I need

First, the imports are for SQLAlchemy, so this is a SQLAlchemy model definitions file. The Base class is the declarative base. The key exports are all the model classes and the Base. The generate_uuid function is used as a default for primary keys. The module is part of the persistence layer, providing ORM models for the backend.

For the module summary: It defines the ORM models using SQLAlchemy's DeclarativeBase. Key exports include ProjectType, Project, Tag, and their relationships. These models structure the database schema for the application's data storage needs.

Now, each symbol. Let's start with Base. It's the base class for all models, using DeclarativeBase. generate_uuid generates UUIDs for primary keys. ProjectType represents project templates with metadata. RecordingTemplate is similar for recordings. Project organizes recordings. Tag is for categorization. RecordingTag is a junction table for many-to-many between recordings and tags.

items.title: encryption.py
items.text:
Credential encryption service using Fernet with OS keychain.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/services/encryption.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/services/encryption.py) — this module (125 lines) Source packages/backend/services/encryption.py Language Python Lines 125 Exports 9

AI Summary

Manages credential encryption using Fernet symmetric keys, storing the master key in the OS keychain or a fallback file. Exports get_master_key(), get_fernet(), encrypt_config(), and decrypt_config() to transparently secure sensitive fields like passwords and tokens in service configurations. Consumed by the configuration loader to encrypt credentials before persistence and decrypt them when services start, ensuring secrets never appear in plain text on disk.

items.title: oauth.py
items.text:
OAuth authentication service for cloud storage providers.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/services/oauth.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/services/oauth.py) — this module (408 lines) Source packages/backend/services/oauth.py Language Python Lines 408 Exports 12

AI Summary

{"module":"Orchestrates the three-legged OAuth flow for Google Drive, OneDrive, and Dropbox by spinning up a local callback server, managing PKCE state, and exchanging codes for refresh tokens. Exports start_oauth() to initiate the flow, get_oauth_status() to poll completion, and exchange_code_for_tokens() to finish the handshake, all consumed by the CLI's login command and the Settings UI. Integrates with core.oauth_credentials to fetch rotating client secrets and stores ephemeral state in memory until the user authorizes or cancels.","get_client_credentials":"Retrieves the client_id and secret for a provider, first checking the encrypted credentials store and falling back to environment variables.","build_auth_url":"Constructs the provider-specific authorization URL with scopes, state, and offline access to begin the OAuth handshake.","exchange_code_for_tokens":"Swaps the authorization code for access and refresh tokens via the provider’s token endpoint and returns the full token payload.","refresh_access_token":"Uses a stored refresh token to obtain a new access token without user interaction, keeping long-lived sync sessions alive.","start_oauth":"Launches a local callback server on an available port, generates a secure state parameter, and returns the authorization URL and state for the user to visit

items.title: exceptions.py
items.text:
Defines custom exception classes for storage-related errors, exposing StorageError and its subclasses for error handling. Key exports include StorageError, StorageUnavailableError, StorageConnectionError, StorageAuthError, StorageNotFoundError, and StoragePermissionError. These exceptions are used throughout the storage adapter to signal specific error conditions. Consumed by storage adapter implementations to handle and propagate errors.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/backend/storage/exceptions.py`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/backend/storage/exceptions.py) — this module (33 lines) Source packages/backend/storage/exceptions.py Language Python Lines 33 Exports 6

items.title: useDocuments.ts
items.text:
{"module":"Manages the document lifecycle through React Query hooks that orchestrate CRUD operations, file uploads, OCR processing, and bulk actions. Exposes useDocuments() for filtered listings, useDocument() for single document access, and mutation hooks like useUploadDocument() and useDeleteDocument() that automatically invalidate related queries. Integrates with the API layer via @/lib/api and coordinates cache invalidation across documents, dashboard stats, and job queries.","useDocuments":"Fetches a paginated document list with optional filters for search, status, date range, and project association.","useDocument":"Retrieves a single document's details by ID and suspends rendering until data loads.","useUploadDocument":"Handles file uploads with optional title, project assignment, and OCR enabling, then refreshes document and dashboard caches.","useUpdateDocument":"Patches a document's title or project assignment and invalidates the document list cache.","useDeleteDocument":"Removes a document with optimistic UI updates that rollback on failure.","useBulkDeleteDocuments":"Deletes multiple documents in one call and invalidates related caches.","useBulkAssignDocuments":"Assigns or removes project associations for a batch of documents.","useRunDocumentOcr":"Triggers OCR processing on a document and monitors the

items.title: useLiveTranscription.ts
items.text:
Okay, let's tackle this. The user wants a JSON summary of the provided TypeScript file. The file is called useLiveTranscription.ts and it's part of the frontend hooks. The main exports are several interfaces and a custom React hook.

First, the module summary needs to explain the architectural role. Looking at the code, it's a React hook for managing live transcription. It uses WebSocket for real-time data, handles media recording, and manages connection states. The key exports are the interfaces and the hook itself. It connects to the API module for URLs and is probably used by components needing transcription features.

For each symbol: - WordData represents individual words with timing and confidence. Used when processing transcription chunks. - TranscriptSegment is a collection of words into a segment with metadata. Used for displaying parts of the transcript. - LiveError handles error states with retryability. Used to manage and display errors during transcription. - UseLiveTranscriptionReturn is the return type of the hook, containing state and functions. It's what components use to interact with the transcription. - useLiveTranscription is the hook that sets up everything, manages state, and connects to WebSocket. Used in components to get transcription functionality.

Need to avoid generic phrases. Instead of saying "pro

items.title: api.ts
items.text:
{"module":"Centralizes all backend communication by orchestrating API URL resolution across Electron IPC, injected variables, and environment fallbacks while managing JWT tokens and generating typed fetch wrappers for every backend endpoint. Exports initializeApiUrl(), getApiUrl(), and 80+ TypeScript interfaces that mirror the backend schema, enabling the UI layers to consume recordings, transcriptions, AI models, and storage resources through a unified client. Consumed by every feature module to ensure consistent auth handling and endpoint routing without hardcoding URLs.","onAuthRequired":"Registers a callback that the fetch interceptor invokes when any API call receives a 401, letting the UI trigger a login flow.","setAuthTokens":"Persists the access token and optional refresh token to localStorage so subsequent API calls include the Authorization header.","clearAuthTokens":"Removes both tokens from localStorage, immediately logging the user out and forcing re-authentication on the next request.","hasAuthToken":"Returns true if an access token exists in localStorage, used by routers to decide whether to show protected pages.","initializeApiUrl":"Determines the correct backend base URL by trying Electron IPC first, then an injected global, then VITE_API_URL, caching the result for the session.","isElectron":"Detects

items.title: utils.ts
items.text:
Centralizes Tailwind CSS class merging via cn() and manages timezone-aware datetime formatting for the frontend. Exports TIMEZONE_OPTIONS, timezone storage functions, and formatDateTime/formatDate/formatTime to convert UTC backend timestamps to user-selected timezones. Consumed by UI components to display localized timestamps and handle timezone preference persistence across sessions.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/frontend/src/lib/utils.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/frontend/src/lib/utils.ts) — this module (188 lines) Source packages/frontend/src/lib/utils.ts Language TypeScript Lines 188 Exports 10

items.title: Exports
items.text:
Exports (10)Internal (2) Name Kind Description cn function Merges Tailwind CSS classes by combining clsx and twMerge for conditional className generation. TIMEZONE_OPTIONS constant TimezoneValue type getStoredTimezone function Retrieves the user's saved timezone preference from localStorage or defaults to 'auto'. setStoredTimezone function Saves the selected timezone to localStorage and dispatches a timezone-change event for reactive UI updates. formatDateTime function Format a date string from the backend (UTC without Z suffix) to the user's selected timezone. formatDate function Format just the date portion. formatTime function Format just the time portion. formatDuration function Format a duration in seconds as MM:SS (or M:SS for compact display). formatRelativeTime function Format a relative time string (e.g., "2 hours ago", "yesterday").
  • TIMEZONE_STORAGE_KEY — constant

Format a date string from the backend (UTC without Z suffix) to the user's selected timezone.

items.title: keybindingStore.ts
items.text:
Central keybinding store — single source of truth for all customizable shortcuts.

Central keybinding store — single source of truth for all customizable shortcuts. Uses Zustand with localStorage persistence. Only user overrides are stored; defaults are implicit and forward-compatible with new actions.

Relevant source files The following files were used as context for this page: - [`packages/frontend/src/stores/keybindingStore.ts`](https://github.com/JongoDB/verbatim-studio/blob/main/packages/frontend/src/stores/keybindingStore.ts) — this module (202 lines) Source packages/frontend/src/stores/keybindingStore.ts Language TypeScript Lines 202 Exports 10

AI Summary

{"module":"Central keybinding store that orchestrates all customizable shortcuts across playback and live recording modes using Zustand with localStorage persistence. Exposes formatCombo(), matchesCombo(), and combosEqual() for keyboard event handling, plus getPlaybackShortcuts() and getLiveShortcuts() for UI display. Consumed by TranscriptPage and LiveTranscriptionPage components to bind user actions to keyboard shortcuts while maintaining forward compatibility through implicit defaults.","KeyCombo":"Defines a keyboard combination with optional modifier keys used throughout the keybinding system.","ActionDef":"Describes a user-actionable command with its category, display label, and default key combination.","formatCombo":"Converts a KeyCombo into a human-readable string like 'Ctrl+Shift+K' or '⌘+Space' for UI display.","matchesCombo":"Determines if a KeyboardEvent matches a specific KeyCombo, handling case-insensitive letter keys and modifier validation.","combosEqual":"Compares two KeyCombo objects for equality, normalizing letter case and boolean modifiers.","getPlaybackShortcuts":"Returns an array of playback shortcuts with formatted key strings and descriptions for display in the transcript page.","getLiveShortcuts":"Returns an array of live recording shortcuts with formatted key strings and descriptions for display in the live
